summary: USMCA's review dominates the day: USTR is preparing a Mexico visit citing progress on non-tariff barriers, while business leader José Medina Mora warns the pact's fate hinges on the 2026 or 2029 U.S. elections. On security, 'El Mayo' Zambada, over 70, awaits a life sentence in the U.S. In finance, Hacienda has missed its deadline to issue rules for the 2025 anti-money-laundering reform, even as Mexican startups raised 944 million dollars last quarter, up 131 percent, extending their lead over Brazil.

lead.h1: USTR prepares visit to Mexico as USMCA review continues
lead.context: The U.S. Trade Representative acknowledged progress on so-called non-tariff barriers ahead of a planned visit to Mexico, part of the ongoing review of the USMCA trade pact.
lead.background: USMCA governs Mexico-US-Canada trade and, since the US declined a 16-year extension at the July 2026 joint review, the pact now faces annual reviews through 2036. Economy Secretary Marcelo Ebrard's team has been negotiating with USTR for months on the underlying trade-barrier disputes.
lead.drivers: Washington is sending negotiators for a third bilateral round, July 21-23 in Mexico City, to advance talks on steel and aluminum, autos, economic security, labor, agriculture and electronic payment services.
lead.implications: Greer says the goal includes closing gaps that let third countries benefit improperly from the treaty, tying the review to non-tariff barriers already flagged in USTR's 2026 report.
lead.next: The two delegations meet July 21-23 in Mexico City to continue the sector-by-sector talks.

items.headline: Finance ministry misses deadline to issue rules under anti-money-laundering reform
items.context: The 2025 reform was billed as the biggest update to Mexico's anti-laundering regime in over a decade, but Hacienda's delay in issuing implementing rules stalls its application.
items.background: The July 2025 reform to Mexico's anti-money-laundering law, LFPIORPI, took effect in March and was meant to tighten oversight of real estate, vehicle sales, jewelry and other 'vulnerable activities' through new beneficial-owner identification and cash-use rules.
items.drivers: Hacienda has not published the general provisions needed to operationalize several of the law's new obligations, including beneficial-owner identification, cash-use limits and new SAT reporting schemes.
items.implications: The regulatory vacuum leaves the vulnerable-activity sectors the reform targeted without the rules needed to comply in practice.

items.headline: BIS warns Banxico and other central banks on excessive debt purchases
items.context: The Bank for International Settlements flagged reputational risk for central banks, including Banxico, that intervene heavily in money markets by buying government debt.
items.background: Banxico sets its policy rate to hold inflation near its 3% target and, like other central banks, sometimes buys government debt in secondary markets to smooth market disruptions. Public debt as a share of GDP has been rising.
items.drivers: The BIS says elevated debt-to-GDP ratios in countries including Mexico complicate managing money supply and interest rates, which can force central banks to intervene more often to fix market dysfunctions.
items.implications: The BIS warns that repeated large-scale asset purchases or credit operations risk creating moral hazard for both financial markets and governments.

items.headline: Business council says USMCA's future hinges on 2026 or 2029 U.S. elections
items.context: CCE leader José Medina Mora made the comments ahead of a third round of negotiations between Mexico and the United States over the trade pact's review.
items.background: USMCA's future is being shaped by the annual review process triggered when the US rejected a 16-year extension, with Trump having floated a possible 10-year extension subject to yearly reviews.
items.drivers: CCE president José Medina Mora says the treaty's direction still depends largely on Trump's own position, based on conversations with US agencies ahead of the third negotiating round.
items.next: If there is no progress after November's US midterm elections, Mexican business leaders are prepared to wait until 2029, when Trump's term ends and a new administration takes office.

items.headline: 'El Mayo' Zambada awaits life sentence in the United States
items.context: The former Sinaloa cartel leader, over 70, faces sentencing on charges of trafficking tons of drugs into the U.S., a case with weight in the bilateral security relationship.
items.background: Zambada co-founded the Sinaloa Cartel with Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán in the late 1980s and was captured in an unusual arrest in July 2024; he has been held in the United States for two years and pleaded guilty months ago.
items.next: His defense has asked that if he receives a life sentence, he be held in a federal prison able to provide him specialized medical care.

items.headline: Mexico's 40-hour workweek reform begins its phased rollout
items.context: The reform cuts the legal workweek from 48 to 40 hours, with a transition starting in 2027, marking one of the country's largest labor changes in decades and a cost factor companies are already planning around.
items.background: Mexico's Federal Labor Law has mandated a 48-hour workweek for decades, among the longest in Latin America, making any cut a structural change for employer cost planning and scheduling.

items.headline: Mexico extends its venture capital lead over Brazil in Latin America
items.context: Mexican startups raised 944 million dollars in the second quarter, up 131 percent from a year earlier, pulling further ahead of Brazil as global venture firms increase their presence in the region.
items.background: Latin America's venture market has been split mainly between Mexico and Brazil, with global funds increasingly active in the region's fintech, logistics and transportation startups.
items.drivers: Investors including Founders Fund and Andreessen Horowitz led some of the quarter's largest deals, and Mexico-based companies took the region's three biggest fundraising deals in Q2.
items.implications: A continued boom in late-stage and growth funding lifted the whole region, with Latin American startups raising $1.36 billion in the quarter, up 47% year over year.

items.headline: Nu eyes payroll banking to challenge BBVA and Santander in Mexico
items.context: Analysts say Nu Mexico's base of 15 million users gives it an opening to compete for payroll accounts, a segment long dominated by legacy banks.
items.background: Nu México just won approval to operate as a bank, entering a market where BBVA and other legacy banks dominate payroll accounts, the anchor product banks use to lock in other business.
items.drivers: Nu points to its 15 million Mexican users to challenge that dominance, and analysts note payroll portability is a worker's right, not the employer's, so better direct terms could shift accounts quickly.
items.implications: Winning payroll clients would strip traditional banks of a cheap financing source tied to their corporate payroll agreements.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.52 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.

live.tmpl: the peso trades at {v} pesos to the dollar
